tagged_story_for_tts:
Hands shaping tools. Tools shaping work.

The phrase "workspace" once meant a cave floor strewn with flint knives—where a single spark could turn stone into blades sharper than lion claws. By 3000 BCE, Egyptian scribes sat cross-legged on reed mats, grinding ink from soot and gum, their laps as desks. The first "offices" were clay tablets: portable, erasable, baked hard when the deal was final.

Factories in 1770s England locked workers to benches for 16-hour shifts, their hands moving like loom shuttles. Then came Taylorism: stopwatches timed every motion, turning humans into machine appendages. Steel desks arrived in 1880, bolted together like train cars—because the same companies built both. The 1960s cubicle, born from a designer’s hate of open-plan chaos, promised privacy but delivered isolation: walls just tall enough to block eye contact, not sound.

Now laptops turn cafés into offices, and algorithms track keystrokes like Taylor’s stopwatch. Yet the oldest workspace rule remains: the best tools disappear into the task—whether it’s a flint knife, a scribe’s lap, or a screen glowing in the dark.

Three related subjects are cave art tools, factory time discipline, and ergonomic failures.
